title: Behavioural Economics
summary: A journalist and a behavioural economist discuss the key insights of behavioural economics — including loss aversion, the endowment effect, and choice architecture — and debate whether findings from the field justify paternalistic policy interventions.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Behavioural economics has gone from an academic curiosity in the 1970s to something that governments and corporations now routinely apply. Can you give listeners who are less familiar with the field a sense of what the core insight is — what does it challenge in standard economic theory?

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: The core insight is deceptively simple: human beings are not the rational, self-interested utility maximisers that classical economic theory assumes. We are predictably irrational — we make systematic errors in judgement that violate the axioms of rational choice theory in ways that are consistent and therefore exploitable. Standard economics treated deviations from rationality as random noise; behavioural economics showed they have identifiable structure.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: And the two figures most associated with establishing that structure academically are Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, with their prospect theory?

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: Exactly. Kahneman and Tversky demonstrated through elegant experimental work in the 1970s and 80s that people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point rather than in absolute terms, and that losses loom larger than equivalent gains. This is loss aversion: the pain of losing twenty dollars is psychologically about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining twenty dollars. It seems simple, but it has enormous implications for how people make financial, medical, and career decisions.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: The endowment effect follows from this, doesn't it? The tendency to place higher value on things you already own simply because you own them?

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: Precisely. Richard Thaler's classic experiments — famously involving coffee mugs — showed that people demand significantly more to give up something they own than they would be willing to pay to acquire the identical object. This violates the Coase theorem's assumption that initial allocations of property don't matter for efficiency, because ownership itself changes perceived value. And this has real consequences — for housing markets, for contract negotiations, and for the way people hold investment portfolios.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Beyond these individual biases, the field has had perhaps its biggest policy impact through the concept of 'choice architecture'. Can you explain what that means?

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: Choice architecture refers to the way in which the design of a decision environment affects the choices people make, often without their awareness. The canonical example is organ donation default rules. Countries with opt-out systems — where you are presumed to consent to donation unless you actively register otherwise — have dramatically higher donation rates than opt-in systems, even though individuals in both systems are nominally free to choose either option. The default is doing the heavy lifting, not deliberate preference.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Thaler and Sunstein popularised this idea with their concept of 'nudging' — designing choice environments that steer people toward better outcomes while preserving freedom of choice. But that framework has attracted real criticism. Is the critique fair?

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: Some of it is fair, and it deserves a direct answer. The deepest criticism is that nudging is inherently paternalistic — it assumes that a policy designer, a government, or a corporation knows better than the individual what outcomes are in that individual's best interest. And since defaults are always chosen by someone, nudge architecture is never neutral. The question of who decides what the 'better outcome' is, and on whose behalf, is a legitimate and serious one.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: There's also the critique from behavioural economists themselves, isn't there, about replication? Several of the landmark findings that underpinned the field have failed to replicate robustly in more recent studies.

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: Yes, and intellectual honesty requires acknowledging that. Priming effects, some versions of the ego depletion hypothesis, and several classic social psychology findings that behavioural economists relied on have come under serious scrutiny in the replication crisis. That said, the core findings — loss aversion, the endowment effect, present bias — have replicated well across cultures and contexts. The field has been appropriately chastened by the replication crisis but not invalidated by it.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Present bias — the tendency to overweight immediate rewards relative to future ones — seems especially relevant to some of the most pressing policy challenges: climate change, pension saving, public health. How does behavioural economics approach those?

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: Present bias explains a lot of the gap between what people say they value — long-term health, financial security, a stable climate — and what they actually do. Automatic enrolment in pension schemes is the clearest policy success story: when employees are automatically enrolled and have to opt out rather than opt in, participation rates rise dramatically, and longitudinal studies show meaningful improvements in retirement wealth. For climate, the picture is more complex because the required behaviour changes are collective rather than individual, and nudges are poorly suited to collective action problems of that scale.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: So where does behavioural economics leave us when it comes to policy? It seems to occupy an uncomfortable space between acknowledging human irrationality and respecting autonomy.

body.speaker: Expert
body.text: That tension is real and unresolved. My own view is that behavioural economics is most defensible and most effective when it is used to help people achieve outcomes they themselves have identified as desirable — saving more, eating better, exercising regularly — rather than when it is used by corporations or governments to steer people toward outcomes that serve institutional interests rather than individual ones. The difference between a nudge that helps you save for retirement and a dark pattern designed to trap you into a subscription you don't want is not just a matter of degree; it's a fundamental difference in whose interests are being served.

annotations.meaningVi: những người tối đa hóa lợi ích
annotations.exampleEn: Classical economic models assume that consumers are rational utility maximisers who always choose the best option for themselves.

annotations.meaningVi: phi lý trí một cách có thể dự đoán được
annotations.exampleEn: Dan Ariely's book argues that humans are not randomly irrational but predictably irrational in systematic ways.

annotations.meaningVi: các tiên đề của lý thuyết lựa chọn hợp lý
annotations.exampleEn: The transitivity of preferences is one of the axioms of rational choice theory that behavioural economics challenges.

annotations.meaningVi: lý thuyết triển vọng
annotations.exampleEn: Prospect theory shows that people weigh potential losses more heavily than equivalent gains.

annotations.meaningVi: sự né tránh mất mát
annotations.exampleEn: Loss aversion explains why people hold onto falling stocks too long: the pain of realising a loss outweighs the rational case for selling.

annotations.meaningVi: điểm tham chiếu
annotations.exampleEn: People assess gains and losses relative to a reference point, often the status quo.

annotations.meaningVi: hiệu ứng sở hữu
annotations.exampleEn: The endowment effect means that sellers consistently price items higher than buyers are willing to pay for the identical object.

annotations.meaningVi: định lý Coase
annotations.exampleEn: The Coase theorem holds that initial property rights allocations don't affect economic efficiency when transaction costs are zero.

annotations.meaningVi: kiến trúc lựa chọn
annotations.exampleEn: Placing fruit at eye level in a cafeteria is a simple example of choice architecture.

annotations.meaningVi: hệ thống không tham gia chủ động (mặc định đồng ý)
annotations.exampleEn: Austria uses an opt-out system for organ donation, resulting in high registration rates.

annotations.meaningVi: đẩy nhẹ (hành vi theo hướng tốt hơn)
annotations.exampleEn: Sending reminder texts to people who have missed a doctor's appointment is a form of nudging.

annotations.meaningVi: có tính gia trưởng, can thiệp vào sự tự do của người khác vì lợi ích của họ
annotations.exampleEn: Critics argue that sugar taxes are paternalistic because they override personal choice about diet.

annotations.meaningVi: khủng hoảng tái lập (không thể tái hiện kết quả nghiên cứu)
annotations.exampleEn: The replication crisis has led researchers to require larger samples and pre-registered hypotheses.

annotations.meaningVi: kiệt sức bản ngã (ý chí bị tiêu hao)
annotations.exampleEn: The ego depletion hypothesis suggested that willpower is a limited resource, but many replication attempts have produced weak results.

annotations.meaningVi: thiên kiến hiện tại (ưu tiên phần thưởng ngay lập tức)
annotations.exampleEn: Present bias leads people to choose a smaller reward today over a larger reward next month.

annotations.meaningVi: đăng ký tự động
annotations.exampleEn: Automatic enrolment in workplace pensions dramatically increased retirement savings rates in the UK.

annotations.meaningVi: nghiên cứu theo chiều dọc (theo dõi theo thời gian)
annotations.exampleEn: Longitudinal studies follow the same participants over years to track long-term outcomes.

annotations.meaningVi: vấn đề hành động tập thể
annotations.exampleEn: Climate change is a classic collective action problem: everyone benefits if emissions fall, but each individual has an incentive to free-ride.

annotations.meaningVi: mẫu tối (thiết kế giao diện gây nhầm lẫn người dùng)
annotations.exampleEn: Making the unsubscribe button difficult to find is a dark pattern that exploits user inattention.

annotations.meaningVi: được cảnh tỉnh, học được bài học
annotations.exampleEn: The field has been chastened by failed replications, leading to more rigorous pre-registration of studies.

annotations.meaningVi: đơn giản một cách đánh lừa (thực ra phức tạp hơn)
annotations.exampleEn: The rule is deceptively simple — applying it consistently requires considerable skill.

grammarNotes.title: Tag Questions for Confirming and Softening Assertions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu hỏi đuôi (tag questions) như 'doesn't it?', 'isn't there?' được dùng trong hội thoại để xác nhận thông tin đã được ngầm hiểu hoặc để mời người đối thoại đồng ý với một quan sát. Ví dụ: 'The endowment effect follows from this, doesn't it?' Đây không phải là câu hỏi thực sự — người hỏi đã biết câu trả lời — mà là một chiến lược diễn ngôn để kiểm tra sự hiểu biết hoặc xây dựng sự đồng thuận. Câu hỏi đuôi khẳng định dùng phụ động từ phủ định; câu hỏi đuôi phủ định dùng phụ động từ khẳng định.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Tag questions ('doesn't it?', 'isn't there?', 'can't they?') invite confirmation of a claim already tentatively asserted. 'The endowment effect follows from this, doesn't it?' signals that the journalist believes the statement is true and is asking the expert to confirm. A positive clause takes a negative tag; a negative clause takes a positive tag. In academic and professional dialogue, tag questions are a key tool for managing conversational turns and building shared understanding.

grammarNotes.title: Concessive Conjunctions: 'That said' and 'even though'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cụm 'That said' (hay 'That being said') được dùng đầu câu để thừa nhận điều vừa được nêu trong khi giới thiệu một lập luận ngược lại hay bổ sung. Ví dụ: 'That said, the core findings — loss aversion, the endowment effect, present bias — have replicated well across cultures and contexts.' Cấu trúc này cho phép người nói thừa nhận một điểm bất lợi (vấn đề tái lập) mà không từ bỏ lập luận chính (giá trị của lĩnh vực). Tương tự, 'even though' giới thiệu một nhượng bộ trong cùng một câu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'That said' (= 'having said that') is a discourse marker that concedes the preceding point while introducing a qualification or counter-claim. 'That said, the core findings have replicated well.' This structure allows the speaker to appear balanced by acknowledging contrary evidence before reasserting a modified version of their position — a hallmark of credible academic and journalistic argument. 'Even though' performs a similar concessive function within a single clause.

translationVi: Nhà báo mở đầu bằng cách đặt câu hỏi về những thách thức cốt lõi của kinh tế học hành vi đối với lý thuyết kinh tế chuẩn. Chuyên gia giải thích rằng con người không phải là những người tối đa hóa lợi ích hợp lý — họ phi lý trí một cách có thể dự đoán được, mắc các lỗi hệ thống có cấu trúc có thể nhận diện được. Kahneman và Tversky đã chứng minh điều này thông qua lý thuyết triển vọng và sự né tránh mất mát: nỗi đau khi mất hai mươi đô la mạnh gấp đôi niềm vui khi được hai mươi đô la. Hiệu ứng sở hữu của Thaler cho thấy mọi người đánh giá cao hơn những gì họ sở hữu chỉ vì sở hữu nó. Kiến trúc lựa chọn — thiết kế môi trường quyết định — có tác động chính sách lớn nhất thông qua các quy tắc mặc định như hệ thống không tham gia chủ động trong hiến tạng. Nhà báo và chuyên gia thảo luận về phê bình đối với 'nudging' (đẩy nhẹ): nó vốn mang tính gia trưởng và không bao giờ trung lập. Cuộc trò chuyện cũng đề cập đến khủng hoảng tái lập và thiên kiến hiện tại — căn bệnh giải thích khoảng cách giữa những gì mọi người nói họ đánh giá cao và những gì họ thực sự làm. Chuyên gia kết luận rằng kinh tế học hành vi có thể bảo vệ được nhất khi giúp mọi người đạt được mục tiêu của chính họ, chứ không phải phục vụ lợi ích của tổ chức.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The expert distinguishes between nudges that help individuals achieve their own goals and 'dark patterns' that serve institutional interests. Is this distinction as clear-cut as the expert implies? Who decides whether an outcome is in an individual's best interest, and what safeguards should exist to prevent behavioural economics from becoming a tool of manipulation rather than empowerment?

questions.prompt: What does the expert identify as the core insight of behavioural economics?
options: People are essentially rational but require more information to make good decisions
Human beings make systematic, predictably irrational errors that violate rational choice theory axioms
Markets are inefficient because of corporate manipulation
Emotions are the primary driver of all economic decisions
questions.explanation: 'We are predictably irrational — we make systematic errors in judgement that violate the axioms of rational choice theory in ways that are consistent and therefore exploitable.'
questions.hint: What word means the opposite of 'random noise' in terms of errors?

questions.prompt: How did standard economics differ from behavioural economics in its treatment of irrational behaviour?
options: Standard economics ignored irrational behaviour; behavioural economics pretended it did not exist
Standard economics treated irrationality as random noise; behavioural economics showed it has identifiable structure
Standard economics studied irrationality extensively; behavioural economics simplified it
Both treated irrational behaviour as a minor footnote
questions.explanation: 'Standard economics treated deviations from rationality as random noise; behavioural economics showed they have identifiable structure.'
questions.hint: What did standard economics treat irrational behaviour as?

questions.prompt: What did Kahneman and Tversky demonstrate about how people evaluate outcomes?
options: People evaluate outcomes in absolute monetary terms
People always prefer certain outcomes to uncertain ones
People evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point, and losses loom larger than equivalent gains
People use rational expected utility calculations for most decisions
questions.explanation: 'people evaluate outcomes relative to a reference point rather than in absolute terms, and that losses loom larger than equivalent gains.'
questions.hint: What two claims are central to prospect theory?

questions.prompt: How does the expert describe loss aversion quantitatively?
options: The pain of losing ten dollars equals the pleasure of gaining twenty dollars
The pain of losing twenty dollars is about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining twenty dollars
Losses and gains of equal size produce equal but opposite emotional responses
The pain of any loss is ten times greater than an equivalent gain
questions.explanation: 'the pain of losing twenty dollars is psychologically about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining twenty dollars.'
questions.hint: What ratio is given for pain of loss versus pleasure of gain?

questions.prompt: What did Richard Thaler's coffee mug experiments demonstrate?
options: People undervalue items they own compared to items they do not
People demand significantly more to give up something they own than they would pay to acquire the identical item
The price of coffee mugs is highly sensitive to market conditions
Ownership has no effect on perceived value in blind conditions
questions.explanation: 'people demand significantly more to give up something they own than they would be willing to pay to acquire the identical object.'
questions.hint: What is the asymmetry between selling price and buying price?

questions.prompt: How does the endowment effect relate to the Coase theorem?
options: It confirms the Coase theorem's predictions about market efficiency
It violates the Coase theorem's assumption that initial property allocations don't matter for efficiency
It extends the Coase theorem to behavioural contexts
It has no relevance to the Coase theorem
questions.explanation: 'This violates the Coase theorem's assumption that initial allocations of property don't matter for efficiency, because ownership itself changes perceived value.'
questions.hint: What assumption of the Coase theorem is violated?

questions.prompt: What is 'choice architecture' as defined in the dialogue?
options: The range of options available to consumers in a market
The way the design of a decision environment affects the choices people make, often without their awareness
The physical layout of retail spaces to encourage purchasing
Government regulation specifying which choices citizens are permitted to make
questions.explanation: 'Choice architecture refers to the way in which the design of a decision environment affects the choices people make, often without their awareness.'
questions.hint: What does choice architecture affect and how?

questions.prompt: What is the canonical example of choice architecture given in the dialogue?
options: Organ donation default rules in opt-out versus opt-in systems
questions.explanation: 'The canonical example is organ donation default rules. Countries with opt-out systems… have dramatically higher donation rates than opt-in systems.'
questions.hint: What medical system illustrates the power of defaults?

questions.prompt: What does the expert identify as the deepest criticism of nudging?
options: Nudging is too expensive for governments to implement at scale
Nudging doesn't work because people adapt to and ignore defaults
Nudging is inherently paternalistic, assuming policy designers know better than individuals what is in their interest
Nudging has been invalidated by the replication crisis
questions.explanation: 'The deepest criticism is that nudging is inherently paternalistic — it assumes that a policy designer, a government, or a corporation knows better than the individual what outcomes are in that individual's best interest.'
questions.hint: What philosophical criticism is levelled at nudging?

questions.prompt: What additional point does the expert make about the neutrality of defaults?
options: Defaults are neutral because they can be easily changed by anyone
Since defaults are always chosen by someone, nudge architecture is never neutral
Neutral defaults are possible when designed by independent experts
The choice of default doesn't significantly affect outcomes
questions.explanation: 'since defaults are always chosen by someone, nudge architecture is never neutral.'
questions.hint: Who always makes the choice of default?

questions.prompt: Which specific findings does the expert say have come under scrutiny in the replication crisis?
options: Loss aversion and the endowment effect
Priming effects, ego depletion, and some classic social psychology findings
Prospect theory and reference point effects
questions.explanation: 'Priming effects, some versions of the ego depletion hypothesis, and several classic social psychology findings that behavioural economists relied on have come under serious scrutiny in the replication crisis.'
questions.hint: Which specific phenomena are named as problematic?

questions.prompt: Which core findings does the expert say have replicated well?
options: Priming, ego depletion, and social proof
Loss aversion, the endowment effect, and present bias
Anchoring, availability heuristic, and framing effects
Status quo bias, sunk cost fallacy, and overconfidence
questions.explanation: 'the core findings — loss aversion, the endowment effect, present bias — have replicated well across cultures and contexts.'
questions.hint: What three findings are explicitly mentioned as robust?

questions.prompt: What does the expert say automatic pension enrolment demonstrates about present bias?
options: People prefer immediate financial security over future planning when given a choice
When employees are automatically enrolled and must opt out, participation rises dramatically and retirement wealth improves
Present bias only affects low-income workers who cannot afford to save
Pension choices are too complex for behavioural interventions to be effective
questions.explanation: 'when employees are automatically enrolled and have to opt out rather than opt in, participation rates rise dramatically, and longitudinal studies show meaningful improvements in retirement wealth.'
questions.hint: What happens to participation rates under automatic enrolment?

questions.prompt: Why does the expert say nudges are poorly suited to climate change?
options: People don't care enough about climate change for nudges to be motivating
Climate change requires individual sacrifice that nudges cannot incentivise
The required behaviour changes are collective rather than individual, and nudges don't suit collective action problems at that scale
Climate policy is too politically controversial for nudge-based interventions
questions.explanation: 'For climate, the picture is more complex because the required behaviour changes are collective rather than individual, and nudges are poorly suited to collective action problems of that scale.'
questions.hint: What type of problem is climate change that nudges cannot address?

questions.prompt: According to the expert, what distinguishes a legitimate nudge from a 'dark pattern'?
options: Legitimate nudges are designed by governments; dark patterns are corporate
Legitimate nudges use opt-in defaults; dark patterns use opt-out defaults
A legitimate nudge helps people achieve their own identified goals; a dark pattern steers them toward outcomes serving institutional interests
Legitimate nudges are transparent to users; dark patterns operate unconsciously
questions.explanation: 'behavioural economics is most defensible… when it is used to help people achieve outcomes they themselves have identified as desirable… rather than when it is used… to steer people toward outcomes that serve institutional interests rather than individual ones.'
questions.hint: Whose interests distinguish a legitimate nudge from a dark pattern?

cloze.template: Behavioural economics has gone from an academic curiosity in the 1970s to something that governments and corporations now routinely apply. Can you give listeners who are less familiar with the field a sense of what the core insight is — what does it challenge in standard economic theory? The core insight is deceptively simple: human beings are not the rational, self-interested utility maximisers that classical economic theory assumes. We are {{b1}} irrational — we make systematic errors in judgement that violate the axioms of rational choice theory in ways that are consistent and therefore exploitable. Standard economics treated deviations from rationality as random noise; behavioural economics showed they have identifiable {{b2}}. And the two figures most associated with establishing that structure academically are Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, with their prospect theory? Exactly. Kahneman and Tversky demonstrated through elegant experimental work in the 1970s and 80s that people evaluate outcomes relative to a {{b3}} point rather than in absolute terms, and that losses loom larger than equivalent gains. This is {{b4}} aversion: the pain of losing twenty dollars is psychologically about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining twenty dollars. It seems simple, but it has enormous implications for how people make financial, medical, and career decisions. The {{b5}} effect follows from this, doesn't it? The tendency to place higher value on things you already own simply because you own them? Precisely. Richard Thaler's classic experiments — famously involving coffee mugs — showed that people demand significantly more to give up something they own than they would be willing to pay to acquire the identical object. This violates the Coase theorem's assumption that initial {{b6}} of property don't matter for efficiency, because ownership itself changes perceived value. And this has real consequences — for housing markets, for contract negotiations, and for the way people hold investment portfolios. Beyond these individual biases, the field has had perhaps its biggest policy impact through the concept of 'choice architecture'. Can you explain what that means? Choice architecture refers to the way in which the design of a decision environment affects the choices people make, often without their {{b7}}. The canonical example is organ donation {{b9}} rules. Countries with opt-out systems — where you are presumed to consent to donation unless you actively register otherwise — have dramatically higher donation rates than opt-in systems, even though individuals in both systems are {{b8}} free to choose either option. The default is doing the heavy lifting, not deliberate preference. Thaler and Sunstein popularised this idea with their concept of 'nudging' — designing choice environments that steer people toward better outcomes while preserving freedom of choice. But that framework has attracted real criticism. Is the critique fair? Some of it is fair, and it deserves a direct answer. The deepest criticism is that nudging is inherently {{b10}} — it assumes that a policy designer, a government, or a corporation knows better than the individual what outcomes are in that individual's best interest. And since defaults are always chosen by someone, nudge architecture is never {{b11}}. The question of who decides what the 'better outcome' is, and on whose behalf, is a legitimate and serious one. There's also the critique from behavioural economists themselves, isn't there, about replication? Several of the landmark findings that underpinned the field have failed to replicate robustly in more recent studies. Yes, and intellectual honesty requires acknowledging that. Priming effects, some versions of the ego depletion hypothesis, and several classic social psychology findings that behavioural economists relied on have come under serious scrutiny in the replication crisis. That said, the core findings — loss aversion, the endowment effect, present bias — have replicated well across cultures and contexts. The field has been appropriately {{b12}} by the replication crisis but not invalidated by it. Present bias — the tendency to overweight immediate rewards relative to future ones — seems especially relevant to some of the most pressing policy challenges: climate change, pension saving, public health. How does behavioural economics approach those? Present bias explains a lot of the gap between what people say they value — long-term health, financial security, a stable climate — and what they actually {{b13}}. Automatic enrolment in pension schemes is the clearest policy success story: when employees are automatically enrolled and have to opt out rather than opt in, participation rates rise dramatically, and longitudinal studies show meaningful improvements in retirement wealth. For climate, the picture is more complex because the required behaviour changes are collective rather than individual, and nudges are poorly suited to collective {{b14}} problems of that scale. So where does behavioural economics leave us when it comes to policy? It seems to occupy an uncomfortable space between acknowledging human irrationality and respecting autonomy. That tension is real and unresolved. My own view is that behavioural economics is most defensible and most effective when it is used to help people achieve outcomes they themselves have identified as desirable — saving more, eating better, exercising regularly — rather than when it is used by corporations or governments to steer people toward outcomes that serve institutional interests rather than individual ones. The difference between a nudge that helps you save for retirement and a {{b15}} pattern designed to trap you into a subscription you don't want is not just a matter of degree; it's a fundamental difference in whose interests are being served.

blanks.explanation: What adverb means in a way that can be foreseen consistently?
What noun means an organised pattern or framework?
What noun describes a baseline used for comparison?
What noun describes the thing people try to avoid in this bias?
What noun means what you already possess or are given?
What noun refers to the distribution of resources or rights?
What noun means conscious knowledge or perception?
What adverb means in name or theory but not necessarily in practice?
What noun refers to the pre-set option that applies unless changed?
What adjective describes an authority treating adults like children for their own good?
What adjective means impartial or without bias?
What past participle means made more humble or cautious by a negative experience?
What simple verb completes the contrast with 'say they value'?
What noun follows 'collective' to name the coordination challenge?
What adjective describes a deceptive UX design pattern?
